elements.sent-2411-001: Feature selection is important for high-dimensional data analysis and is non-trivial in unsupervised learning problems such as dimensionality reduction and clustering.
elements.sent-2411-002: The goal of unsupervised feature selection is finding a subset of features such that the data points from different clusters are well separated.
elements.sent-2411-003: This paper presents a novel method called K-means Derived Unsupervised Feature Selection (K-means UFS).
elements.sent-2411-004: Unlike most existing spectral analysis based unsupervised feature selection methods, we select features using the objective of K-means.
elements.sent-2411-005: We develop an alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) to solve the NP-hard optimization problem of our K-means UFS model.
elements.sent-2411-006: Extensive experiments on real datasets show that our K-means UFS is more effective than the baselines in selecting features for clustering.
elements.sent-2411-007: The selected features should preserve the most important information of the data for downstream tasks such as classification and clustering.
elements.sent-2411-008: Many unsupervised feature selection methods have been proposed in the past decades.
elements.sent-2411-009: They can be organized into three categories \cite{alelyani2013feature, khalid2014survey : filter methods \cite{langley1994selection , wrapper methods \cite{kohavi1997wrappers , and hybrid methods.}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: Filter methods evaluate the score of each feature according to certain criteria, such as Laplacian score (LS) \cite{he2005laplacian,zhao2007spectral and scatter separability criterion \cite{dy2004feature .}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Wrapper methods utilize algorithms to evaluate the quality of selected features.
elements.sent-2411-012: They repeat selecting a subset of features and evaluating the performance of an algorithm on these features until the performance of the algorithm is desired.
elements.sent-2411-013: Correlation-based feature selection \cite{hall1999feature and Gaussian mixture models \cite{constantinopoulos2006bayesian are representative ones of wrapper methods.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Hybrid methods \cite{das2001filters, liu2005toward utilize filtering criteria to select feature subsets and evaluate the feature subsets by algorithm performance.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Without labels to evaluate the feature relevance, many criteria have been proposed for unsupervised feature selection in recent years.
elements.sent-2411-016: The most widely used one is to select features that can preserve the data similarity using Laplacian matrix.
elements.sent-2411-017: For instance, Multi-Cluster Feature Selection (MCFS) \cite{cai2010unsupervised selects features using spectral analysis and a regression model with $\ell_1$-norm regularization.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Non-negative Discriminative Feature Selection (NDFS) \cite{li2012unsupervised selects features using non-negative spectral analysis and a regression model with $\ell_{2,1}$-norm regularization.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Robust Unsupervised Feature Selection (RUFS) \cite{qian2013robust selects features using label learning, non-negative spectral analysis and a regression model with $\ell_{2,1}$-norm regularization.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Joint Embedding Learning and Sparse Regression (JELSR) \cite{hou2013joint selects features using embedding learning and sparse regression jointly.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Li et al. \cite{li2013extremely developed a sampling scheme called feature generating machines (FGMs) to select informative features on extremely high-dimensional problems.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Non-negative Spectral Learning and Sparse Regression-based Dual-graph regularized feature selection (NSSRD) \cite{shang2017non extends the framework of joint embedding learning and sparse regression by incorporating a feature graph.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Wang et al. \cite{wang2021autoweighted proposed to select features using an autoweighted framework based on a similarity graph.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Embedded Unsupervised Feature Selection (EUFS) \cite{wang2015embedded used sparse regression and spectral analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Li et al. \cite{li2021sparse proposed an unsupervised feature selection method based on sparse PCA with $\ell_{2,p}$-norm.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Sparse and Flexible Projection for Unsupervised Feature Selection with Optimal Graph (SF$^2$SOG) \cite{wang2022sparse selects features using the optimal flexible projections and orthogonal sparse projection with $\ell_{2,0}$-norm constraint.}
elements.sent-2411-027: All these methods select features based on data similarity using spectral analysis, but they don't focus on the separability of data points under the selected feature space.
elements.sent-2411-028: In this work, we present a new method called K-means Derived Unsupervised Feature Selection (K-means UFS).
elements.sent-2411-029: Unlike those spectral analysis based methods, we select features to minimize the K-means objective proposed by \cite{ding2004k, zha2001spectral .}
elements.sent-2411-030: The goal of our method is to select the most discriminative features such that the data points are well separated, that is, have small within-cluster differences and large between-cluster differences.
elements.sent-2411-031: We focus on the separability of data points and derive this new unsupervised feature selection method from K-means.
elements.sent-2411-032: The contributions of this work are as follows.
elements.sent-2411-033: A novel unsupervised feature selection method is proposed to select the most discriminative features based on the objective of K-means.
elements.sent-2411-034: An Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (ADMM) \cite{boyd2011distributed algorithm is developed for the NP-hard problem of K-means UFS model.}
elements.sent-2411-035: We compare K-means UFS with other state-of-the-art unsupervised feature selection methods and conduct experiments on real datasets to demonstrate the effectiveness of our method.
elements.sent-2411-036: The rest of this paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-037: In Section \ref{sec_2 , we derive the K-means UFS model from K-means objective.}
elements.sent-2411-038: In Section \ref{sec_opt , we develop an ADMM algorithm to solve the optimization problem of our K-means UFS model.}
elements.sent-2411-039: In Section \ref{sec_4 , we compare K-means UFS with other state-of-art unsupervised feature selection methods in detail.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Section \ref{sec_5 presents the experiments and Section \ref{sec_conclusion draws conclusions for this paper.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Table \ref{tab_notation shows the main notations used in this paper.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \caption{Notations}\label{tab_notation} \begin{tabular}{l|l} symbol description $\bm{x}$ column vector $\bm{x}_i$ column vector with index $i$ $X$ matrix $Tr(\cdot)$ trace of matrix $\|\cdot\|_2$ $\ell_2$ or Euclidean norm of vector $\|\cdot\|_F$ Frobenius norm of matrix $\mathbbm{1}(\cdot)$ $\mathbbm{1}(x) = 1$ if $x \neq 0$; $\mathbbm{1}(x) = 0$ if $x=0$ $\Vert \cdot \Vert_{2, 1}$ \makecell{$\ell_{2,1}$ norm of matrix, defined as: \qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad $\Vert M \Vert_{2, 1} = \sum_{j=1}^q \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^p M^2_{ij}} = \sum_{j=1}^q \Vert \bm{m}_j \Vert_2$} $\Vert \cdot \Vert_{2, 0}$ \makecell{$\ell_{2,0}$ norm of matrix, defined as:\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad $\Vert M \Vert_{2, 0} = \sum_{j=1}^q \mathbbm{1}\Big(\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^p M^2_{ij}}\Big) = \sum_{j=1}^q \mathbbm{1}(\Vert \bm{m}_j \Vert_2)$} \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-042: In unsupervised feature selection, there is no unique criteria to evaluate the quality of selected features.
elements.sent-2411-043: We choose to select features by minimizing the objective of K-means clustering proposed by \cite{ding2004k, zha2001spectral .}
elements.sent-2411-044: Given a data matrix $X = (\bm{x_1 , \bm{x_2 , ..., \bm{x_n ) \in \mathbb{R ^{p \times n}$, where $p$ denotes the number of features and $n$ denotes the number of samples.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: Each feature (row) of $X$ is standardized to have zero mean and unit variance.
elements.sent-2411-046: In K-means clustering, the $k$ centroids are determined by minimizing the sum of squared errors, \begin{equation}\label{eqn:k-means_obj_0} \begin{aligned} J_k = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \sum_{i \in C_j} \left\lVert \bm{x_{i}} - \bm{m_j}\right\rVert_2^2, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\bm{m_j = \sum_{i \in C_j \bm{x_{i / n_j$ is the centroid of cluster $C_j$ consisting of $n_j$ points, $j=1,\ldots,k$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: According to \cite{ding2004k , $J_k$ can be reformulated as \begin{equation}\label{eqn:k-means_obj} \begin{aligned} J_k = \text{Tr}(X^\top X) - \text{Tr}(G^\top X^\top X G), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $G = (\bm{g_1 , \dots, \bm{g_k )\in\mathbb{R ^{n\times k}$ is a normalized indicator matrix denoting whether a data point is in a cluster or not, namely, \begin{equation}\label{eqn:indicator_G} g_{ij}=\left\lbrace \begin{array}{cc} 1/\sqrt{n_j}, \text{if}~\bm{x}_i\in C_j 0, \text{otherwise}. \end{array} \right.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:k-means_obj}  J_k = Tr(X^\top X) - Tr(G^\top X^\top X G), \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-048: Let $\Psi $ be the feasible set of all possible indicator matrices with $k$ clusters.
elements.sent-2411-049: Since $\text{Tr (X^\top X)$ is a constant, \cite{ding2004k pointed out that K-means clustering is equivalent to \begin{equation}\label{eqn:k-means_problem} \begin{aligned} \min_{G \in \Psi} ~ - \text{Tr}(G^\top X^\top X G). \end{aligned}\end{equation} Our K-means derived unsupervised feature selection (K-means UFS) seeks to select the most discriminative features.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: The input data $X$ contains $p$ rows of features.
elements.sent-2411-051: Let $X_h \in \mathbb{R ^{h \times n}$ be the selected $h$ rows of $X$.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Our feature selection goals is the following: {\bf Among all possible choice of $X_h$, we selection the $X_h$ which minimizes the K-means objective .}
elements.sent-2411-053: Therefore, K-means UFS solves the following problem \begin{equation}\label{eqn:feature_selection_for_k-means_problem_1} \begin{aligned} \min_{X_h} ~ [\min_{G \in \Psi} ~- \text{Tr}(G^\top X_h^\top X_h G)] \end{aligned}\end{equation} In order to show the intuition of model \eqref{eqn:feature_selection_for_k-means_problem_1 , we generate a toy data matrix $X \in \mathbb{R ^{4 \times 30}$, shown in Figure \ref{fig:diff_X_h .}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: The left plot shows $X_{h1}$ consisting of the first two rows of $X$, while the right one shows $X_{h2}$ consisting of the last two rows of $X$.
elements.sent-2411-055: We prefer $X_{h2}$ because the within-cluster differences are much smaller than those in $X_{h1}$.
elements.sent-2411-056: It is expected that solving problem \eqref{eqn:feature_selection_for_k-means_problem_1 can select the last two features.}
elements.sent-2411-057: We want to select the most discriminative features in an unsupervised manner.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.85\columnwidth,trim={0 0 0 5},clip]{./figures/diff_X_h.png} \caption{Visualization of $X_{h1}$ and $X_{h2}$ of a toy example} \label{fig:diff_X_h} {(Description: The x-axis and y-axis of the left figure represent the first two features (rows) of $X$, while the axis of the right one represent the last two features of $X$.)}
elements.sent-2411-058: In this section, we show that problem \eqref{eqn:feature_selection_for_k-means_problem_1 can be formulated into a discrete quadratic optimization problem.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Let $S \in \mathbb{R ^{p \times h}$ be a selection matrix defined as: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} X_h = S^\top X,~~\text{s.t.}~ S^\top S=I, ~~ S_{ij} \in \{0, 1\} \end{aligned} \label{eqn:define_S}\end{equation} Let $\Phi$ be the feasible set of all the selection matrix $S$, we rewrite problem \eqref{eqn:feature_selection_for_k-means_problem_1 as: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:feature_selection_for_k-means_problem_2} \begin{aligned} \min_{G \in \Psi,S\in\Phi}- \text{Tr}(S^\top X G G^\top X^\top S) \end{aligned}\end{equation} Therefore \eqref{eqn:feature_selection_for_k-means_problem_2 solves both the K-means clustering problem (the optimal $G$) and the feature selection problem (optimal $S$) simultaneously.}}}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation} X_h = S^\top X,~~s.t.~ S^\top S=I, ~~ S_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}  \label{eqn:define_S}\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:feature_selection_for_k-means_problem_2}  \min_{G \in \Psi,S\in\Phi}- Tr(S^\top X G G^\top X^\top S) \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-061: Fortunately, the approximate solution to K-means clustering was obtained in \cite{ding2004k, zha2001spectral .}
elements.sent-2411-062: The approximate solution of K-means indicator $G^*$ can be constructed as the following.
elements.sent-2411-063: Let the singular value decomposition (SVD) of $X$ be $X = P \Sigma Q^\top$, where $P$ and $Q$ denote the left and right singular vectors and the singular values in $\Sigma$ are sorted decreasingly.
elements.sent-2411-064: Let $P_k$ be the first $k$ columns of $P$, $Q_k$ be the first $k$ columns of $Q$ and $\Sigma_k$ be the first $k$ singular values of $\Sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-065: Now, with this approximate optimal solution for $G$, we rewrite the objective of problem \eqref{eqn:feature_selection_for_k-means_problem_2 as \begin{equation}\min_{S\in\Phi}~ - Tr(S^\top P \Sigma Q^\top Q_k Q_k^\top Q \Sigma P^\top S)\end{equation} then, the feature selection optimization problem \eqref{eqn:feature_selection_for_k-means_problem_2 is simplified to \begin{equation}\min_{S\in\Phi}~ -\text{Tr}(S^\top P_k \Sigma_k^2 P_k^\top S) \equiv -\text{Tr}(S^\top A S) \label{eq:1}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}A = P_k \Sigma_k^2 P_k\end{equation} is in fact the largest $k$ rank of the square of data covariance matrix.}}
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}\min_{S\in\Phi}~ - Tr(S^\top P \Sigma Q^\top Q_k Q_k^\top Q \Sigma P^\top S)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-066: Problem \eqref{eq:1 is our model for K-means UFS.}
elements.sent-2411-067: Problem \eqref{eq:1 is a discrete optimization problem which is typically NP-hard.}
elements.sent-2411-068: To solve this problem we use numerical relaxation.
elements.sent-2411-069: From Eq.(6), we infer that $S$ has $h$ nonzero rows.
elements.sent-2411-070: This can be seen as the following.
elements.sent-2411-071: The $p$ rows of $X$ can be reordered such that the selected rows are reshuffled to the top $h$ rows of $X$, \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} X = \begin{bmatrix} X_h X_{-h} \end{bmatrix}, S = \begin{bmatrix} I_{h \times h} \bm{0}_{(p-h) \times h} \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $X_{-h}$ represents the rest of rows un-selected, $I_{h \times h}$ is an identity matrix and $\bm{0 _{(p-h) \times h}$ is a zero matrix.}
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation} X =  X_h X_{-h} , S =  I_{h \times h} 0_{(p-h) \times h}  \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-072: Therefore, $S$ has three constraints: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} S^\top S = I,~ \Vert S^\top \Vert_{2,0} = h,~~ S_{ij} \in \{0, 1\} \end{aligned} \label{eq:2}\end{equation} Now, from the K-means UFS model \eqref{eq:1 , if $S^*$ is an optimal solution, $V=S^* R$ (where $R \in \mathbb{R ^{h \times h}$ is a rotation matrix, that is, $R R^\top = I$.) is also an optimal solution, because $\text{Tr (S^\top A S) = \text{Tr (R^\top S^\top A S R)$.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-012: \begin{equation} S^\top S = I,~ \Vert S^\top \Vert_{2,0} = h,~~ S_{ij} \in \{0, 1\}  \label{eq:2}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-073: Thus the binary discrete constraint in Eq.\eqref{eq:2 is not necessary.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Therefore, the relaxed $V$ has only two constraints: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} V^\top V = I,~ \Vert V^\top \Vert_{2,0} = h \end{aligned} \label{eq:3}\end{equation} Finally, we solve the K-means UFS model by the following relaxed optimization problem. \begin{equation}\label{eqn:L20_problem} \begin{aligned} \min_V \quad -\text{Tr}(V^\top A V) s.t. \quad V^\top V = I,~ \Vert V^\top \Vert_{2,0} = h \end{aligned}\end{equation} Note that once the optimal solution $V^*$ is obtained, the feature selection matrix $S^*$ is determined uniquely by the index of the $h$ nonzero rows of $V^*$.
elements.formula-2411-014: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:L20_problem}  \min_V \quad -Tr(V^\top A V) s.t. \quad V^\top V = I,~ \Vert V^\top \Vert_{2,0} = h \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-075: The value of rotation matrix $R$ has no contribution to feature selection.
elements.sent-2411-076: In this section, we elaborate how to solve optimization problem \eqref{eqn:L20_problem using ADMM.}
elements.sent-2411-077: We consider an equivalent form of problem \eqref{eqn:L20_problem : \begin{equation}\label{eqn:L20_problem_ADMM} \begin{aligned} \min_V\quad -\text{Tr}(V^\top A V) s.t. \quad V = U, \quad U^\top U = I V = W, \quad \lVert W^\top \Vert_{2,0} = h. \end{aligned}\end{equation} Then the augmented Lagrangian function of \eqref{eqn:L20_problem_ADMM is \begin{equation}\label{eqn:L20_problem_ADMM_aug_Lagrangian} \begin{aligned} L_{\mu}(V, U, W) = -\text{Tr}(V^\top A V) + \frac{\mu }{2} \Vert V - U + \Omega/\mu \Vert^2_F + \frac{\mu }{2} \Vert V - W + \Gamma/\mu\Vert^2_F + \text{const} \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\Omega,\Gamma \in \mathbb{R ^{p \times h $ are Lagrange multiplier matrices, $\mu > 0$ is a penalty parameter, $U$ is an orthogonal matrix and $W$ is a row-sparse matrix.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-015: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:L20_problem_ADMM}  \min_V\quad -Tr(V^\top A V) s.t. \quad V = U, \quad U^\top U = I V = W, \quad \lVert W^\top \Vert_{2,0} = h. \end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-016: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:L20_problem_ADMM_aug_Lagrangian}  L_{\mu}(V, U, W) = -Tr(V^\top A V) + \frac{\mu }{2} \Vert V - U + \Omega/\mu \Vert^2_F + \frac{\mu }{2} \Vert V - W + \Gamma/\mu\Vert^2_F + const \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-078: Then we update the variables alternately \cite{boyd2011distributed : \begin{equation}V^{t+1} = \argmin_V L_{\mu}(V, U^{t}, W^{t}) \label{eqn:admm_update_V} U^{t+1} = \argmin_U L_{\mu}(V^{t+1}, U, W^{t}) \label{eqn:admm_update_U} W^{t+1} = \argmin_W L_{\mu}(V^{t+1}, U^{t+1}, W) \label{eqn:admm_update_W} \Omega^{t+1} = \Omega^{t} + \mu^\top (V^{t+1} - U^{t+1}) \label{eqn:admm_update_Omega} \Gamma^{t+1} = \Gamma^{t} + \mu^\top (V^{t+1} - W^{t+1}) \label{eqn:admm_update_Gamma} \mu^{t+1} = \mu^\top \times \rho , \quad \rho = 1.05. \label{eqn:admm_update_mu}\end{equation} Step 1: Update $V$ Let $B = U^\top - \frac{\Omega^\top {\mu^\top}$, $C = W^\top - \frac{\Gamma^\top {\mu^\top}$, after algebra, the update $V$ step \eqref{eqn:admm_update_V is solving the following problem: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:V_step_problem} \min_V\quad -\text{Tr}(V^\top A V) + \frac{\mu^\top }{2} \left\lVert V - B \right\rVert^2_F + \frac{\mu^\top }{2} \left\lVert V - C \right\rVert^2_F\end{equation} Take the derivative of this objective function to be zero, we can update $V$ in $t$ iteration by: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:solution_of_V_step_problem} V^{t+1} = \frac{1}{2} \mu^\top(\mu^\top I - A)^{-1}(B + C)\end{equation} To guarantee the minimal solution exists, $(\mu^\top I - A)$ should be a positive definite matrix.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-017: \begin{equation}V^{t+1} = \argmin_V L_{\mu}(V, U^{t}, W^{t}) \label{eqn:admm_update_V} U^{t+1} = \argmin_U L_{\mu}(V^{t+1}, U, W^{t}) \label{eqn:admm_update_U} W^{t+1} = \argmin_W L_{\mu}(V^{t+1}, U^{t+1}, W) \label{eqn:admm_update_W} \Omega^{t+1} = \Omega^{t} + \mu^\top (V^{t+1} - U^{t+1}) \label{eqn:admm_update_Omega} \Gamma^{t+1} = \Gamma^{t} + \mu^\top (V^{t+1} - W^{t+1}) \label{eqn:admm_update_Gamma} \mu^{t+1} = \mu^\top \times \rho , \quad \rho = 1.05. \label{eqn:admm_update_mu}\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-018: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:V_step_problem} \min_V\quad -Tr(V^\top A V) + \frac{\mu^\top }{2} \left\lVert V - B \right\rVert^2_F + \frac{\mu^\top }{2} \left\lVert V - C \right\rVert^2_F\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-079: Suppose $\lambda_1$ is the max eigenvalue of $A$, we select the initial value of $\mu$ as $\mu^0 = \lambda_1 + 0.1$.
elements.sent-2411-080: Step 2: Update $U$ The update $U$ step \eqref{eqn:admm_update_U is solving the following problem: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \min_{U}\quad \frac{\mu^\top }{2} \Vert V^{t+1} - U + \Omega^\top/\mu^\top \Vert^2_F s.t. \quad U^\top U = I \end{aligned}\end{equation} Let $D = V^{t+1 + \Omega^\top/\mu^\top$, it is equivalent to: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:U_step_orthogonal_problem} \begin{aligned} \max_U\quad \text{Tr}(D^\top U) s.t. \quad U^\top U = I \end{aligned}\end{equation} Let $P_h$ be the first $h$ column of $P$ from $D = P \Sigma Q^\top$ (SVD).}}
elements.formula-2411-020: \begin{equation} \min_{U}\quad \frac{\mu^\top }{2} \Vert V^{t+1} - U + \Omega^\top/\mu^\top \Vert^2_F s.t. \quad U^\top U = I \end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-021: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:U_step_orthogonal_problem}  \max_U\quad Tr(D^\top U) s.t. \quad U^\top U = I \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-081: The solution is: \begin{equation}\label{alg:ADMM_update_U} \begin{aligned} U^{t+1} = P_h Q^\top \end{aligned}\end{equation} Step 3: Update $W$ Let $F = V^{t+1 + \Gamma^\top/\mu^\top$, the update $W$ step \eqref{eqn:admm_update_W is solving the following problem: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:ADMM_MV_W_step_problem} \begin{aligned} \min_{W} \quad \Vert F - W \Vert^2_F s.t. \quad \Vert W^\top \Vert_{2,0} = h \end{aligned}\end{equation} Now we split $W$ and $F$ by rows: \begin{equation}W^\top = [w^{1T}, w^{2T}, \dots, w^{pT}] F^\top = [f^{1T}, f^{2T}, \dots, f^{pT}]\end{equation} Select $\bm{l = \{l_1, l_2, ..., l_h\}$ as the subset of $h$ row indices in $F$ satisfy: \begin{equation}\Vert f^{l_1} \Vert_2 \ge \Vert f^{l_2} \Vert_2 \ge \dots \ge \Vert f^{l_h} \Vert_2 \ge \Vert f^{j} \Vert_2,~~\forall j \notin l\end{equation} Thus, we update each row of $W$ in $t$ iteration by: \begin{equation}\label{alg:ADMM_update_W} \begin{aligned} \bm{w}^{(t+1)j} = \bm{f}^j, \quad \forall j \in \bm{l} \bm{w}^{(t+1)j} = \vec{0}, \quad \forall j \notin \bm{l} \end{aligned}\end{equation}.}}}
elements.formula-2411-025: \begin{equation}\Vert f^{l_1} \Vert_2 \ge \Vert f^{l_2} \Vert_2 \ge \dots \ge \Vert f^{l_h} \Vert_2 \ge \Vert f^{j} \Vert_2,~~\forall j \notin l\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-026: \begin{equation}\label{alg:ADMM_update_W}  w^{(t+1)j} = f^j, \quad \forall j \in l w^{(t+1)j} = \vec{0}, \quad \forall j \notin l \end{equation}
elements.figure-2411-003: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.8\columnwidth, trim={20 8 35 35},clip]{./figures/log_history_norm_V.png} \caption{$\log_{10}(\Vert V\Vert_F)$ of Quadratic ADMM Eq. \eqref{eqn:L20_problem_ADMM} and Bi-linear ADMM Eq. \eqref{eqn:L20_problem_ADMM_linear}.} \label{fig2:ADMM_log_l1_norm_V_h_8} {(Description: The y-axis represents the value of $\log_{10}(\Vert V\Vert_F)$ and the x-axis represents the iteration of ADMM. The $\Vert V\Vert_F$ of Quadratic ADMM Eq. \eqref{eqn:L20_problem_ADMM} increases into $10^{12.5}$ in 80 iterations.)} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-082: In Figure \ref{fig2:ADMM_log_l1_norm_V_h_8 , we apply Quadratic ADMM Eq.\eqref{eqn:L20_problem_ADMM on StatLog DNA data\cite{king1995statlog ($3186$ samples, $180$ features and $3$ classes) and obsverve the $\log_{10 (\Vert V\Vert_F)$ (blue) increases into $12.5$ before convergence, that means the scale of matrix $V$ is blow-up.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: The very large values in $V$ will dominate the ADMM process.
elements.sent-2411-084: It's caused by the subproblem \eqref{eqn:admm_update_V in update $V$ step.}
elements.sent-2411-085: The $(\mu I - A)$ matrix is an ill-conditioned matrix for some data and $\mu$, thus we should {\bf NOT compute the the inversion of it in Eq. \eqref{eqn:solution_of_V_step_problem .}}
elements.sent-2411-086: In next subsection, two useful tricks are presented to avoid the scale blow-up problem.
elements.sent-2411-087: The first trick to avoid scale blow-up is to force $\Vert V \Vert_F$ to be a constant in each iteration of ADMM.
elements.sent-2411-088: Considering $V$ is an orthogonal matrix in Eq. \eqref{eqn:L20_problem , the norm of $V$ should satisfy the constraint: \begin{equation}\Vert V \Vert_F^2 = \text{Tr}(V^\top V) = \text{Tr}(I_{h \times h}) = h\end{equation} The second trick is to change the quadratic objective function ($-\text{Tr (V^\top A V)$) into a bi-linear one ($-\text{Tr (V^\top A U)$).}}}
elements.formula-2411-027: \begin{equation}\Vert V \Vert_F^2 = Tr(V^\top V) = Tr(I_{h \times h}) = h\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-089: This trick can avoid the matrix inversion in Eq.\eqref{eqn:solution_of_V_step_problem .}
elements.sent-2411-090: Using these two tricks, we obtain an equivalent bi-linear form of Quadratic ADMM Eq.\eqref{eqn:L20_problem_ADMM as following: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:L20_problem_ADMM_linear} \begin{aligned} \min_V\quad -\text{Tr}(V^\top A U) s.t. \quad \Vert V \Vert_F^2 = h, \quad V = U, \quad U^\top U = I V = W, \quad \Vert W^\top \Vert_{2,0} = h \end{aligned}\end{equation} The $\Vert V \Vert_F^2 = h$ is necessary for Bi-linear ADMM, though it is redundant in math.}
elements.formula-2411-028: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:L20_problem_ADMM_linear}  \min_V\quad -Tr(V^\top A U) s.t. \quad \Vert V \Vert_F^2 = h, \quad V = U, \quad U^\top U = I V = W, \quad \Vert W^\top \Vert_{2,0} = h \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-091: We can see it in the update $V$ step.
elements.sent-2411-092: Step 1: Update $V$ Let $B = U^\top - \frac{\Omega^\top {\mu^\top}$ and $C = W^{t - \Gamma^{t /\mu^\top $, the update $V$ step of problem \eqref{eqn:L20_problem_ADMM_linear is solving the following problem: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:updata_V_linear} \begin{aligned} \min_V\quad -\text{Tr}[(U^{tT} A + \mu^\top B^\top + \mu^\top C^\top) V] s.t. \quad \Vert V \Vert_F^2 = h \end{aligned}\end{equation} Let $D = U^{tT A + \mu^\top B^\top + \mu^\top C^\top$, the solution is: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:ADMM_MV_solution_of_V} V^{t+1} = \frac{\sqrt{h} }{\left\lVert D\right\rVert_F} D\end{equation} The $\Vert V \Vert_F^2 = h$ constraint is necessary to guarantee the minimal solution for problem \eqref{eqn:updata_V_linear exits.}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-029: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:updata_V_linear}  \min_V\quad -Tr[(U^{tT} A + \mu^\top B^\top + \mu^\top C^\top) V] s.t. \quad \Vert V \Vert_F^2 = h \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-093: In each iteration, updating $V$ by Eq. \eqref{eqn:ADMM_MV_solution_of_V will force $\Vert V\Vert_F^2$ to be a constant $h$.}
elements.sent-2411-094: In Figure \ref{fig2:ADMM_log_l1_norm_V_h_8 , the $\log_{10 (\Vert V\Vert_F)$ (red) of Bi-linear ADMM is always a constant.}}
elements.sent-2411-095: Step 2: Update $U$ Let $E = V^{t+1 + \Omega^\top/\mu^\top$, after algebra, the update $U$ step of Bi-linear ADMM is solving the following problem: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \min_{U}\quad -\text{Tr}(V^{(t+1)T} A U) + \frac{\mu^\top}{2}\Vert U - E \Vert^2_F s.t. \quad U^\top U = I \end{aligned}\end{equation} Let $H = A V^{t+1 + \mu E$, $P_h$ be the first $h$ column of $P$ from $ H = P \Sigma Q^\top$ (SVD).}}
elements.formula-2411-031: \begin{equation} \min_{U}\quad -Tr(V^{(t+1)T} A U) + \frac{\mu^\top}{2}\Vert U - E \Vert^2_F s.t. \quad U^\top U = I \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-096: The solution is: \begin{equation}\label{alg:ADMM_MV_update_U} \begin{aligned} U^{t+1} = P_h Q^\top \end{aligned}\end{equation} The update $W$ step is exactly the same as Eq. \eqref{alg:ADMM_update_W , so we omit it here.}
elements.sent-2411-097: With these update rules, our K-means UFS using Bi-linear ADMM is summarized in Algorithm \ref{alg:ADMM .}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{K-means UFS via Bi-linear ADMM} \label{alg:ADMM} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE Data $X$, number of clusters $k$, number of selected features $h$. \STATE Initialize $\mu = 0.1$, $\rho = 1.05$. $V, U, W$ is initialized using Eq. \eqref{eqn:initialze_V}. $A$ is initialized using Eq. \eqref{eq:1}. \STATE Set $t = 0$ \REPEAT \STATE Update $V$ using Eq.\eqref{eqn:ADMM_MV_solution_of_V}. \STATE Update $U$ using Eq.\eqref{alg:ADMM_MV_update_U}. \STATE Update $W$ using Eq.\eqref{alg:ADMM_update_W}. \STATE Update $\Omega$, $\Gamma$ and $\mu$ using Eq.\eqref{eqn:admm_update_Omega}, Eq.\eqref{eqn:admm_update_Gamma}, Eq.\eqref{eqn:admm_update_mu}. \UNTIL{Convergence} Select the $h$ features determined by the $h$ nonzero rows in $W$. \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-098: We initialize $V$ by removing the discrete $\ell_{2,0}$-norm constraint in problem \eqref{eqn:L20_problem : \begin{equation}\label{eqn:initialze_V} \min_V \quad -\text{Tr}(V^\top A V)~~~s.t. \quad V^\top V = I\end{equation} Let $P_h$ be the first $h$ column of $P$ from $A = P \Sigma P^\top$ (SVD), then we initialize $V = P_h$.}
elements.sent-2411-100: If $\mu$ is larger than $\mu_{max = 10^7$, we stop updating $\mu$.}
elements.sent-2411-101: The convergence proof of ADMM can be found in \cite{boyd2011distributed .}
elements.sent-2411-102: In ADMM process, $W$ is always a row-sparse matrix with $h$ nonzero rows (Eq. \eqref{alg:ADMM_update_W ) which represents the $h$ selected features.}
elements.sent-2411-103: In $t$ iteration, we can recover a unique feature selection matrix $S^\top$ determined by the $h$ nonzero row indices of $W^\top$ because of $W^\top = V^\top$ and $V^\top = S^\top R$.
elements.sent-2411-104: Our termination criteria is: {\bf If $S^\top$ doesn't change in 30 iterations, we stop the ADMM and output the selected features.}
elements.sent-2411-105: In practice, we set the maximum iteration value as $3000$.
elements.sent-2411-106: In experiment, our algorithm converges within 300 iterations for all datasets.
elements.sent-2411-107: The time complexity of update $V$ step \eqref{eqn:ADMM_MV_solution_of_V involves the computation of $D$ and its Frobenius norm, which is both $\mathcal{O (np)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-108: The time complexity of update $U$ \eqref{alg:ADMM_MV_update_U involves computation of $E$ and its SVD, which is $\mathcal{O (np)$ and $\mathcal{O (n p^2)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: The time complexity of update $W$ Eq.\eqref{alg:ADMM_update_W involves computation of $F$ and sort the norm of rows in $F$, which is $\mathcal{O (np)$ and $\mathcal{O (p \log(p))$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-110: Since $n \gg p$, the time complexity of each iteration is $\mathcal{O (n p^2)$.}
elements.sent-2411-111: There are no adjustable parameters in the relaxed K-means UFS model \eqref{eq:3 .}
elements.sent-2411-112: The two parameters $\mu$ and $\rho$ come from the ADMM.
elements.sent-2411-113: If we set $\rho = 1.05$ and using the initialization settings above.
elements.sent-2411-114: Our algorithm is a deterministic algorithm with reproducibility for all the datasets we used.
elements.sent-2411-115: In this section, we compare K-means UFS with other state-of-the-art unsupervised feature selection (UFS) methods.
elements.sent-2411-116: Most other UFS methods select features using spectral analysis \cite{he2005laplacian, zhao2007spectral and sparse regression.}
elements.sent-2411-117: They tends to select features that can preserve the structure of similarity matrix.
elements.sent-2411-118: Our K-means UFS \eqref{eqn:feature_selection_for_k-means_problem_1 select features in a totally different way.}
elements.sent-2411-119: We select the most discriminative features using the K-means objective which have smaller within-cluster difference and larger between-cluster difference.
elements.sent-2411-120: For convenience, we denote original data $X \in \mathbb{R ^{p \times n}$, similarity matrix\footnote{Different methods use different definition of $\tilde{S}$ and details can be found in their papers.}}
elements.sent-2411-122: In NDFS and RUFS, they use the k-nearest similarity matrix $\tilde{S}$.
elements.sent-2411-123: Denote $\mathcal{N _k(i)$ as the set of k-nearest nodes of $\bm{x_i}$, then \begin{equation}\tilde{S}_{ij} = \exp{\Vert x_i - x_j \Vert^2 / \sigma},~ x_i \in N_k(j)~or~x_j \in N_k(i) 0,~ otherwise \end{equation} $\tilde{S \in \mathbb{R ^{n \times n}$, $\bm{1 = [1, ..., 1]^\top$, degree matrix $D = \text{diag (\tilde{S \bm{1 )$, Laplacian matrix $L = D - \tilde{S}$, normalized Laplacian matrix $\hat{L = D^{-\frac{1 {2 (D - \tilde{S ) D^{-\frac{1 {2 }$, and $k$ is the number of clusters.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-034: \begin{equation}\tilde{S}_{ij} = \exp{\Vert x_i - x_j \Vert^2 / \sigma},~ x_i \in N_k(j)~or~x_j \in N_k(i) 0,~ otherwise \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-124: Let $Y \in \mathbb{R ^{n \times k}$ be the cluster indicator matrix, $W \in \mathbb{R ^{p \times k}$ be the regression coefficient weights matrix, $V \in \mathbb{R ^{p \times k}$ be the latent feature matrix.}}}
elements.sent-2411-125: Nonnegative Discriminative Feature Selection NDFS \cite{li2012unsupervised selects features using non-negative spectral analysis and sparse regression with $\ell_{2,1}$-norm regularization.}
elements.sent-2411-126: They propose the NDFS model as following: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:NDFS_reg} \begin{aligned} \min_{Y, W}~ Tr(Y^\top \hat{L} Y) + \alpha \Vert W^\top X - Y^\top \Vert_F^2 + \beta \Vert W^\top \Vert_{2,1} \text{s.t. } Y^\top Y = I,~~Y \ge 0 \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are parameters.
elements.formula-2411-035: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:NDFS_reg}  \min_{Y, W}~ Tr(Y^\top \hat{L} Y) + \alpha \Vert W^\top X - Y^\top \Vert_F^2 + \beta \Vert W^\top \Vert_{2,1} s.t.  Y^\top Y = I,~~Y \ge 0 \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-127: Robust Unsupervised Feature Selection RUFS\cite{qian2013robust selects features using non-negative matrix factorization of $X$, non-negative spectral analysis and sparse regression with $\ell_{2,1}$-norm.}
elements.sent-2411-128: They propose the RUFS model as following: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:RUFS_reg} \begin{aligned} \min_{Y, C, W}\quad \Vert X - C Y^\top \Vert_{2,1} + \nu Tr(Y^\top \hat{L} Y) + \alpha \Vert W^\top X - Y^\top \Vert_{2,1} + \beta \Vert W^\top \Vert_{2,1}, \text{s.t. }~~ Y^\top Y = I,~~Y \ge 0,~~ C \ge 0 \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $C \in \mathbb{R ^{p \times k}$ are cluster centers and $\nu$, $\alpha$ $\beta$ are parameters.}
elements.formula-2411-036: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:RUFS_reg}  \min_{Y, C, W}\quad \Vert X - C Y^\top \Vert_{2,1} + \nu Tr(Y^\top \hat{L} Y) + \alpha \Vert W^\top X - Y^\top \Vert_{2,1} + \beta \Vert W^\top \Vert_{2,1}, s.t. ~~ Y^\top Y = I,~~Y \ge 0,~~ C \ge 0 \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-129: Embedded Unsupervised Feature Selection EUFS\cite{wang2015embedded also selects features using non-negative matrix factorization of $X$, non-negative spectral analysis and sparse regression with $\ell_{2,1}$-norm.}
elements.sent-2411-130: They propose the EUFS model as following: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:EUFS_reg} \begin{aligned} \min_{Y, W}\quad \Vert X - V Y^\top \Vert_{2,1} + \nu Tr(Y^\top \hat{L} Y) + \beta \Vert V^\top \Vert_{2,1} \text{s.t. }~~ Y^\top Y = I,~~Y \ge 0 \end{aligned}\end{equation} Sparse and Flexible Projection for Unsupervised Feature Selection with Optimal Graph SF$^2$SOG \cite{wang2022sparse applies spectral analysis on the optimal graph $G \in \mathcal{R ^{n \times n}$, not similarity matrix $S$.}}
elements.formula-2411-037: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:EUFS_reg}  \min_{Y, W}\quad \Vert X - V Y^\top \Vert_{2,1} + \nu Tr(Y^\top \hat{L} Y) + \beta \Vert V^\top \Vert_{2,1} s.t. ~~ Y^\top Y = I,~~Y \ge 0 \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-131: Denote $L_G \in \mathcal{R ^{n \times n}$ as the normalized Laplacian matrix of $G$.}
elements.sent-2411-132: They propose the SF$^2$SOG model as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:SFSOG_reg} \begin{aligned} \min_{Y, W, G}~ Tr(Y^\top \hat{L}_G Y) + \lambda \Vert W^\top X - Y^\top \Vert_F^2 + \gamma \Vert G - S \Vert_F^2 \text{s.t. } W^\top W = I, \Vert W^\top \Vert_{2,0} = h, 0 \le G_{ij} \le 1, \sum_{j=1}^n G_{ij} = 1 \end{aligned}\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-038: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:SFSOG_reg}  \min_{Y, W, G}~ Tr(Y^\top \hat{L}_G Y) + \lambda \Vert W^\top X - Y^\top \Vert_F^2 + \gamma \Vert G - S \Vert_F^2 s.t.  W^\top W = I, \Vert W^\top \Vert_{2,0} = h, 0 \le G_{ij} \le 1, \sum_{j=1}^n G_{ij} = 1 \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-133: NDFS \eqref{eqn:NDFS_reg , RUFS \eqref{eqn:RUFS_reg , EUFS \eqref{eqn:EUFS_reg and SF$^2$SOG \eqref{eqn:SFSOG_reg are all based on the spectral analysis and sparse regression framework.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-134: Our K-means UFS are totally different from these methods.
elements.sent-2411-135: NDFS, RUFS, EUFS and SF$^2$SOG consider the local structure of data distribution using spectral analysis and sparse regression.
elements.sent-2411-136: The $\ell_{2,1}$-norm regularization used in NDFS, RUFS and EUFS can be regarded as a relaxation of $\ell_{2,0}$-norm constraints\cite{zhang2014feature .{\bf Third , K-means UFS reveals the relationship between data separability and the largest $k$ rank of the square of data covariance matrix $A$ in Eq. \eqref{eq:1 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-137: Researchers can design more advanced methods base on our K-means objective criterion to select features that can increase data separability.
elements.sent-2411-138: In this section, we conduct experiments to evaluate the performance of K-means UFS.
elements.sent-2411-139: Following previous unsupervised feature selection work\cite{zhao2007spectral, li2012unsupervised, cai2010unsupervised , we only evaluate the performance of K-means UFS in terms of clustering.}
elements.sent-2411-140: We evaluate the performance of clustering using accuracy(ACC) and Normalized Mutual Information(NMI).
elements.sent-2411-141: The experiments are conducted on six real datasets.
elements.sent-2411-142: Some datasets are too large so we choose a small subset of them to reduce the time costs of Laplacian matrix computation ($\mathcal{O (n^2)$) in Laplacian Score, NDFS, RUFS and EUFS.}
elements.sent-2411-143: The statistics of the datasets are summarized in Table \ref{table:dataset .}
elements.sent-2411-144: MicroMass\footnote{MicroMass is in UCI Machine Learning Repository: \url{https://archive.ics.uci.edu/ml/datasets/MicroMass is a dataset for the identification of microorganisms from mass-spectrometry data.}}
elements.sent-2411-145: Human Activity Recognition using Smartphones (HARS)\footnote{HARS is in UCI Machine Learning Repository: \url{https://archive.ics.uci.edu/ml/datasets/human+activity+recognition+using+smartphones is a dataset for the recognition of human activities using waist-mounted smartphone with embedded inertial sensors.}}
elements.sent-2411-146: The original dataset consists of 10299 samples so we select the first 500 samples in each classes.
elements.sent-2411-147: Fashion-MNIST (Fashion)\footnote{Fashion-MNIST is a dataset for Kaggle competitions : \url{https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/zalando-research/fashionmnist is a dataset of Zalando's article images consisting of 70000 samples including T-shirts, Trousers and so on.}}
elements.sent-2411-148: We select the first 300 samples in each classes.
elements.sent-2411-149: Gina\footnote{Gina is a benchmark dataset in IJCNN 2007 Workshop on Agnostic Learning vs. Prior Knowledge: \url{http://www.agnostic.inf.ethz.ch/datasets.php is a benchmark dataset for handwritten digit recognition in the agnostic learning.}}
elements.sent-2411-150: Fabert and Dilbert\footnote{\url{https://automl.chalearn.org/data are benchmark datasets for multiclass tasks in AutoML challenge\cite{guyon2019analysis .}}}
elements.sent-2411-151: Fabert contains 8237 samepls so we select the first 500 samples in each classes.
elements.sent-2411-152: Dilbert contains 10000 samepls so we select the first 700 samples in each classes.
elements.figure-2411-004: \caption{Statistics of the Datasets} \label{table:dataset} \begin{tabular}{c|c|c|c} Dataset $\#$ of samples $\#$ of features $\#$ of classes MicroMass 1300 360 10 HARS 3000 561 6 Fashion 3000 784 10 Gina 3468 784 10 Fabert 3500 800 7 Dilbert 3500 2000 5 \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-153: In experiments, the numbers of selected features are set as $\{50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300\}$ for all datasets.
elements.sent-2411-154: There are no adjustable parameters in K-means UFS model need to be tuned.
elements.sent-2411-155: In experiments, we set $\rho = 1.05$ and use the initialization settings above, the ADMM (Algorithm \ref{alg:ADMM ) is a deterministic algorithm with reproducibility for all the datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-156: We compare K-means UFS with the following unsupervised feature selection methods:.
elements.sent-2411-157: All Features: All original features are adropted.
elements.sent-2411-162: SF$^2$SOG: Sparse and Flexible Projection for Unsupervised Feature Selection with Optimal Graph \eqref{eqn:SFSOG_reg \cite{wang2022sparse.}}
elements.sent-2411-163: There are some parameters to be set for baseline methods.
elements.sent-2411-164: Following \cite{qian2013robust, wang2015embedded , we set the neighborhood size to be 5 for the similarity matrix of all datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-165: To fairly compare different unsupervised feature selection methods, the $\alpha,\beta,\gamma, \lambda$ parameters in RUFS \eqref{eqn:RUFS_reg , EUFS \eqref{eqn:EUFS_reg , SF$^2$SOG \eqref{eqn:SFSOG_reg are tuned by "grid-search" strategy from $\{10^{-6 , 10^{-4 , ..., 10^{4 , 10^{6}\}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-166: We report the best clustering results from the optimal parameters.
elements.sent-2411-167: Following \cite{li2012unsupervised, qian2013robust, wang2015embedded , we use K-means to cluster samples based on the selected features.}
elements.sent-2411-168: Since K-means depends on initialization, we repeat the clustering 20 times with random initialization and report the average results with standard deviation.
elements.figure-2411-005: \caption{Clustering Results(ACC $\%$ $\pm$ std) of Different Feature Selection Algorithms} \label{tab:acc} \textwidth{!}{ \setlength{\tabcolsep}{1.2mm} \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{0.8} \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|} Datasets All Features Laplacian Score NDFS RUFS EUFS SF$^2$SOG K-means UFS MicroMass 48.1 $\pm$ 4.47 49.2 $\pm$ 4.58 51.1 $\pm$ 1.75 50.6 $\pm$ 3.61 50.4 $\pm$ 2.89 51.6 $\pm$ 2.73 \bf 52.3 $\pm$ 5.65 HARS 44.6 $\pm$ 0.70 46.1 $\pm$ 0.29 46.2 $\pm$ 1.15 47.2 $\pm$ 0.21 46.7 $\pm$ 0.16 47.1 $\pm$ 1.32 \bf 47.4 $\pm$ 0.17 Fashion 50.0 $\pm$ 1.46 52.1 $\pm$ 1.20 53.2 $\pm$ 3.34 53.3 $\pm$ 1.88 53.8 $\pm$ 2.42 53.0 $\pm$ 1.24 \bf 54.1 $\pm$ 1.00 Gina 48.0 $\pm$ 1.58 46.0 $\pm$ 2.38 48.1 $\pm$ 0.14 49.5 $\pm$ 2.42 46.9 $\pm$ 0.67 49.1 $\pm$ 1.25 \bf 50.1 $\pm$ 0.42 Fabert 17.3 $\pm$ 0.51 17.1 $\pm$ 0.90 17.8 $\pm$ 0.61 17.3 $\pm$ 0.72 17.3 $\pm$ 0.75 17.5 $\pm$ 1.32 \bf 18.0 $\pm$ 0.49 Dilbert 35.1 $\pm$ 0.28 37.2 $\pm$ 0.39 37.9 $\pm$ 0.28 37.6 $\pm$ 0.19 37.3 $\pm$ 0.09 37.1 $\pm$ 2.10 \bf 38.0 $\pm$ 0.57 \end{tabular}}
elements.figure-2411-006: \caption{Clustering Results(NMI $\%$ $\pm$ std) of Different Feature Selection Algorithms} \label{tab:nmi} \textwidth{!}{ \setlength{\tabcolsep}{1.2mm} \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{0.8} \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|} Datasets All Features Laplacian Score NDFS RUFS EUFS SF$^2$SOG K-means UFS MicroMass 58.7 $\pm$ 3.53 59.3 $\pm$ 3.84 61.6 $\pm$ 1.05 61.2 $\pm$ 2.75 61.3 $\pm$ 1.92 61.7 $\pm$ 1.11 \bf 62.3 $\pm$ 5.59 HARS 46.6 $\pm$ 0.40 47.3 $\pm$ 0.55 47.7 $\pm$ 0.82 48.1 $\pm$ 0.12 48.2 $\pm$ 0.06 47.1 $\pm$ 1.22 \bf 48.4 $\pm$ 0.12 Fashion 49.7 $\pm$ 0.62 50.3 $\pm$ 0.56 51.2 $\pm$ 1.90 52.1 $\pm$ 0.66 51.9 $\pm$ 0.72 52.2 $\pm$ 1.26 \bf 52.5 $\pm$ 0.34 Gina 43.4 $\pm$ 1.14 40.0 $\pm$ 1.22 42.2 $\pm$ 0.17 44.3 $\pm$ 1.25 41.9 $\pm$ 0.42 43.1 $\pm$ 1.14 \bf 44.8 $\pm$ 0.34 Fabert 2.9 $\pm$ 1.13 2.7 $\pm$ 1.12 3.4 $\pm$ 0.55 3.0 $\pm$ 0.60 3.3 $\pm$ 0.98 3.5 $\pm$ 1.28 \bf 3.6 $\pm$ 0.77 Dilbert 13.6 $\pm$ 0.30 14.6 $\pm$ 0.12 16.9 $\pm$ 0.24 16.4 $\pm$ 0.18 15.7 $\pm$ 0.02 16.1 $\pm$ 1.22 \bf 17.5 $\pm$ 1.08 \end{tabular}}
elements.sent-2411-169: We list the experimental results of different methods in Table \ref{tab:acc and Table \ref{tab:nmi .}}
elements.sent-2411-170: From these two tables, we have the following observations.
elements.sent-2411-171: It can reduce the number of features significantly and improve the clustering performance.
elements.sent-2411-172: This UFS critierion is effective and is different from other spectral analysis based criteria such as NDFS and RUFS.
elements.sent-2411-173: This can be mainly explained by the following reasons.
elements.sent-2411-174: First, K-means UFS mainly focus on the discriminative information information which results in more accurate clustering.
elements.sent-2411-175: Second, \cite{ding2004k, zha2001spectral pointed out that the approximate solution $G^*$ \eqref{eq:1 to K-means clustering can preserve the structure information of data $X$.}}
elements.sent-2411-176: Third, the $\ell_{2,0}$-norm constraint in k-means UFS can reduce the redundant and noisy features.
elements.figure-2411-007: \subfloat[ACC for MicroMass ($\rho = 1.05$)]{ \includegraphics[width=0.48\columnwidth, trim={18 8 30 10},clip]{./figures/acc_mu.png}} \subfloat[NMI for MicroMass ($\rho = 1.05$)]{ \includegraphics[width=0.48\columnwidth, trim={20 8 30 10},clip]{./figures/nmi_mu.png}} \subfloat[ACC for MicroMass ($\mu^0 = 0.1$)]{ \includegraphics[width=0.48\columnwidth, trim={10 8 30 10},clip]{./figures/acc_rho.png}} \subfloat[NMI for MicroMass ($\mu^0 = 0.1$)]{ \includegraphics[width=0.48\columnwidth, trim={18 8 30 10},clip]{./figures/nmi_rho.png}} \caption{ACC and NMI of K-means UFS with different initial $\mu^0$ and $\rho$ on MicroMass data} \label{fig:param_sens} {(Description: In figure(a)(b), we fix $\rho$ then tune $\mu^0$. In figure(c)(d), we fix $\mu^0$ then tune $\rho$.)}
elements.figure-2411-008: \subfloat[MicroMass]{ \includegraphics[width=0.48\columnwidth, trim={18 8 30 10},clip]{./figures/MicroMass_converges.png}} \subfloat[HARS]{ \includegraphics[width=0.48\columnwidth, trim={20 8 30 10},clip]{./figures/HARS_converges.png}} \caption{Convergence curve of K-means UFS on MicroMass and HARS data ($\mu^0 = 0.1, \rho = 1.05, h = 50$)} \label{fig:convergence} {(Description: The x-axis represents iterations and the y-axis represents objective value of K-means UFS in ADMM.)}
elements.sent-2411-177: We also study the sensitiveness of parameters and the convergence of ADMM.
elements.sent-2411-178: There are no adjustable parameters need to be tuned in our K-means UFS model Eq. \eqref{eq:1 .}
elements.sent-2411-179: We tune the initial value of penalty parameter $\mu^0$ from $\{10^{-6 , 10-^{-3 , ..., 10^6\}$ and its update coefficient $\rho$ from $\{1.0001, 1.01, 1.1, 10, 100\}$ in ADMM (Algorithm \ref{alg:ADMM ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-180: Due to the space limit, we only report experiments over MicroMass.
elements.sent-2411-181: Fig. \ref{fig:param_sens show that when $\mu^0 \le 1$ and $\rho \le 1.1$, our method is not sensitive to $\mu^0$ and $\rho$.}
elements.sent-2411-182: If $\mu^0 \ge 10^3$ or $\rho \ge 10 $, the ADMM will converge within few iterations, which damages the clustering performance.
elements.sent-2411-183: Therefore, we choose $\mu^0 = 0.1$ and $\rho = 1.05$ in experiments.
elements.sent-2411-184: Fig. \ref{fig:convergence is the convergence curves of K-means UFS over MicroMass and HARS.}
elements.sent-2411-185: Our method will converges very quickly and selects a subset of features with low K-means objective value.
elements.sent-2411-186: In this paper, we propose a novel unsupervised feature selection method called K-means UFS.
elements.sent-2411-187: Our method selects the most discriminative features by minimizing the objective value of K-means.
elements.sent-2411-188: We derive a solvable K-means UFS model with $\ell_{2,0}$-norm using the approximate indicators of K-means and numerical relaxed trick.
elements.sent-2411-189: We also develop an ADMM algorithm for K-means UFS.
elements.sent-2411-190: The K-means objective criterion for UFS is totally different from the most widely used spectral analysis criterion.
elements.sent-2411-191: Experiments on real data validate the effectiveness of our method.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}
